danger_signals.visual_form: boss creatures at end of each stage, distinctly inhuman and disturbing in appearance
danger_signals.meaning: environmental hazard and combat challenge ahead

danger_signals.visual_form: excessive violence in gameplay presentation, visceral combat effects
danger_signals.meaning: hostile supernatural threat presence; danger is pervasive

danger_signals.visual_form: disturbing visual design consistent with horror aesthetic
danger_signals.meaning: world state is corrupted/dangerous; supernatural threat active

danger_signals.visual_form: regional variant - Western: skull-like design; Japanese: white mask with black eye stripes (kabuki-inspired)
danger_signals.meaning: primary antagonistic force; source of supernatural corruption

danger_signals.visual_form: burnt-out mansion remains as first stage environment
danger_signals.meaning: evidence of prior catastrophic event; danger zone indicator

safety_signals.visual_form: second mansion (hidden house) appears structurally preserved compared to ruins
safety_signals.meaning: objective location; goal destination where Jennifer can be saved

safety_signals.visual_form: scattered weapons throughout levels available for player use
safety_signals.meaning: player empowerment; reduction of immediate threat through equipment

safety_signals.visual_form: movement from one two-dimensional stage to next
safety_signals.meaning: advancement toward goal; world state transition

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Terror Mask worn by player character
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Mask grants superhuman appearance and combat capability
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player presence marked by destruction and blood trail through levels

faction_indicators.color_identity: Western version: skull-white with dark eye sockets; Japanese version: white with black stripes
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: The mask itself is primary sigil; represents supernatural corruption
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Appears as distinct visual object separate from player, communicates via dialogue
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Presence causes nightmares, haunting, corruption of normal world-state
Located in 'land of the dead' (hidden house); her screams referenced in opening narrative

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Grotesque monster designs; horror-based visual taxonomy
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each stage culminates in unique boss monster
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Distributed throughout eight stages; thematic to stage environment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Charred ruins of West Mansion visible in opening scroll; environmental decay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rick haunted by nightmares; passive state until mask reappears

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Terror Mask reappears to Rick; triggers transition to active gameplay world
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rick accepts mask's temptation; begins progression toward hidden house

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hidden house location; distinct from ruins; appears as intact but ominous structure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Jennifer screams heard in opening; immobilized/imprisoned state requiring rescue

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Eight distinct stages with increasing supernatural intensity; grotesque boss creatures
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rick moves deeper into corrupted territory; combat intensity increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: House sinks into river bottom; removal of corrupted structure from normal world

environmental_storytelling.example: Opening scroll reveals charred West Mansion ruins (past tragedy) then pans to reveal intact hidden house (present objective), establishing narrative progression without dialogue

environmental_storytelling.technique: Regional design variation as narrative tool
environmental_storytelling.example: Western Terror Mask design (skull-like, more overtly evil) vs. Japanese design (kabuki-inspired, more ambiguous) shapes player interpretation of threat nature

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss monster design as stage thematization
environmental_storytelling.example: Each stage's grotesque boss creature visually encodes stage environment type and threat level
Scattered weapons throughout stages telegraph combat intensity ahead; lack of weapons signals relative safety
Transition from burnt ruins (grey/blackened tones) to intact mansion (presumably less corrupted visually) indicates movement toward objective

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: communicates with Rick through spoken temptation; offers power in exchange for action
grants Rick superhuman combat capability; enables progression through dangerous stages
passive, plagued by nightmares; vulnerable to mask's temptation
aggressive combat capability; progresses toward objective despite moral compromise
high-damage combat entities; require player mastery of combat mechanics to defeat
stationary objective; audible presence through screams in narrative; provides motivation

notes: Splatterhouse 2 communicates world-state primarily through visual horror design and architectural contrast. The core manifestation pattern is moral corruption (Rick accepting the mask's power to save Jennifer) visualized through grotesque aesthetics and supernatural enemies. Regional design differences (mask appearance) indicate localization affected threat perception. The game operates on a binary safety model: corrupted/dangerous supernatural realm vs. normal world restoration. No faction diplomacy or political complexity present; world-state is fundamentally binary (corrupted or restored). The opening cinematics and stage progression form the primary narrative communication system beyond combat visuals.
